steps.title: Read the ironic praise
steps.content: The narrator first makes Sir Edmund Vale sound impressive: his features seem suited for a bust or stamp, and his forehead appears full of intelligence. Those details give him the look of a distinguished public figure with gravitas.

steps.title: Notice the missing record
steps.content: The praise becomes ironic when the narrator says it would be impertinent to ask too closely about the actual achievements produced by Sir Edmund's half-century of public service. His memoirs also delay any clarifying details so long that the narrator jokes they might as well remain state secrets.

steps.title: Avoid invented claims
steps.content: Choice B claims he served capably and modestly, but the text questions rather than confirms accomplishment. Choice C invents dishonorable actions, and Choice D assumes genuine achievements are being hidden. Choice A matches the contrast between distinguished appearance and uncertain accomplishment. The answer is A.
